sentences.text: Next happened, it was amazing.
But I had heard that my aunt was treated much better.
Evidently, evidently, that old witch believed me when I told her I could have killed.
Oh, beating up on her, not giving her her rations of food.
Oh, you could, you could kill somebody with harassment like that.
As a matter of fact, many people died on the hand-- by the hands of other prisoners.
There, there were some kapos, let's say, in Auschwitz who, who really enjoyed murdering other prisoners.
There were, for instance-- I, I once saw where two prison-- two prisoner kapos put one-- a long pole, which is sort of like a long baseball bat, on the throat of one prisoner.
And both of them stood on one-- each of them stood on one end of it until, until the, the person died.
They were also going around and hitting people.
Yeah.
It was-- also, they were going around just hitting people indiscriminately.

Let's say when we lined up our food, which was horrible-- line-- and not enough of it either-- who-- when we were crowding around, they started-- they sort of took one of those great big baseball bats and started hitting people in the head.
I, I once got hit in the head.
And I didn't wake up till the following day.
We, we almost always got a piece of what they called bread every day and something that they called either coffee or tea.
And nobody could tell the difference.
And then we got soup.
And some of us became very adept at what we called organizing.
Like, I had a, a, a can that I attached a wire to.
And when the-- some of the prisoners were carrying the soup in large kettles, another guy and I-- one of us always was the decoy.
He would run over and make as if he was going to dip his can in the, in the big pot of soup.
And then they would start chasing him while I would run over and actually dip my can in the soup.
And we would share it.

Occasionally when there wasn't-- when they didn't take you out on a work detail, they would, they would have a group of prisoners, let's say, pick rocks from one pile of rocks and carry it a couple of hundred yards down and deposit it in one place.
And then they had another group of prisoners take the same rocks and bring them back to the original place to sort of make work and make torture and things like that.
Yes.
One of my-- of the most vivid stories about Auschwitz was-- that used to give me nightmares, really.
The place was always cold.
As a matter of fact, I, I don't ever remember being warm in any concentration camp.
I guess they didn't have any summer in those places.
You'd wake-- I'd wake up in the morning before dawn, because that's where we had to stand on appell, where they counted us.
And there was an electrified fence surrounding the, the our camp.
And in the morning, you would take a look and see a bunch of people standing sort of with their arms outstretched, holding on to the electrified fence.
See, those are the people who couldn't take it anymore.
And, it was always foggy.
And you would see-- it was very surrealistic, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a bunch of people standing with their arms outstretched, holding onto an electrified fence.
And it was, it was an easy way to go, I suppose.
No, well, anything else I remember is probably locked in my mind.
They were, they were looking for skilled prisoners to work in, in many places in Germany.
You see Auschwitz at the-- was still in Poland.
And they were looking for prisoners who were skilled and strong enough to work in factories in Germany.
And because my father was a skilled mechanic and I was his helper, they picked us to go to a place called Braunschweig.
Brunswick in Germany.
No-- in North Germany.
B-R-A-U-N-S-C-W-S-C-H-W-E-I-G, Braunschweig.
And--
Must have been about '42, '43 or so.
And again, it was a very long and bad trip on-- in locked cars.
I mean, you would assume that because they needed us to be slave laborers, they would treat us better.
Well, that was, that was not their agenda.
Also, a lot of people died on the way between Auschwitz and Braunschweig.
It was a very long trip.
And it was awfully cold.
Must have been probably more than a week in those locked cars.
Several hundreds-- and a lot of people died, some of them from exposure and cold.
That trip was-- is one of those things that almost was almost blotted from my mind.
I don't even remember what else happened, except occasionally, my father would give me a piece of my bread ration and with some water.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we arouse-- arrived in Braunschweig, which was a work concentration camp.
It was there for--
Yes, my father was still there.
And that's where there, there were some good things happening.
The Allies, the Americans and English in particular, were bombing Germany.
And we liked it, even though some of us were killed.
As a matter of fact, in, in Braunschweig, we were working in a place called the diesel bunker.
We were producing engines for trucks.
This is diesel-- D-I-E-S-E--
D-I-E-S-E-L.
It was called a bunker-- B-U-N-K-E-R.
Because it was an, an underground factory.
And we worked the night shift, 12 hours night shift.
And every time there was an air raid, they would turn off the lights.
And we were made to sit-in a corner, which was a lot of fun.
Because we were resting.
It was very hard work, particularly-- my father was working as a mechanic.
I was a mechanic's helper, which means I had to move those heavy motors.
I became quite adept at it.
I mean, they, they weigh several pounds, very heavy cast iron motors.
And I sort of teeter tottered them from one station to another.
And when another guy who was doing the same kind of work-- the motor dropped.
And he lost several toes.
And he was never seen again.
Also, they told us if we-- if one of those motors dropped, they were cast iron.
And if they, if they dropped, some of them busted.
They fractured.
And that was also a death sentence.
So we had, we had reason to be good workers.
Also, we were working with French prisoners of war in that place.
And we were not allowed to talk to them.
But there was one prisoner.
And they were getting rations of beer.
You know, in Germany, they used to drink beer like in this country drink coffee or orange juice.
And one of the prisoners used to leave me part of his beer.
He would put it on the table, point at it.
And I became quite a, a good beer drinker.
As a matter of fact, that may have saved my life.
Because beer is about as nutritious as, as anything that I was getting in those days.
Also, one of the few-- this was one of the kindnesses that I had experienced in concentration camp.
Another kindness that I experienced also happened in Braunschweig when we were marching from work to-- from our barracks to work.
It was bitter cold.
And I was in the-- to the rear of the column.
And we were marching through town.
So if the-- if, if the Germans say they didn't know there were any concentration camps, they must have closed their eyes very tightly.
And a little old lady dressed in black-- a black hat and a black overcoat threw a brown paper bag at me.
And I caught it and put it underneath my blouse.
And later, we were allowed to go to the bathroom once during a shift.
I went-- when they took us to, to the outhouse, I opened the bag.
And there was a slice of pound cake.
It was, it was more dear than any gold you could possibly imagine.
Anyhow, I told my father what I had.
My father was still alive.
And we cut it up into small pieces and traded it for bread with other prisoners who had, who hadn't seen any cake in years.
Again, that may have saved my life.

But the downside of it was-- my father, we were working, we were, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were taken out to town where we cleaned-- cleared out ruins that had been-- of houses that had-- buildings that had been bombed.
And my father got sick.
And they took him to the dispensary.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I went to visit him.
And he was dead.
And I was talking to one-- there was a guy, a French a doctor, also a prisoner.
And I asked him, how did-- what happened to my father?
And he says he was, he was given an injection.
And I said, an injection of what?
And he told me air.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my father was killed by air.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's when I became very lonely.
Do you know why they did that?
He was a good worker.
He was sick.
He was useless.
Besides, there was nothing purposeful about killing people.
It was senseless.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
You know, it was-- recently, I saw the movie And a friend of mine had seen it before.
And he asked me what I thought of it.
And I said, it, in its place, it's a very good movie.
And I said, Bill, what did you think-- he's a retired attorney, by the way-- I said, Bill, what did you think of it?
And he says, there was too much senseless killing.
Now, here's a very knowledgeable man.
And he thought it was-- there was too much senseless killing.
I said-- he says, they could have toned it down a little bit.
I said, this camp that they showed was actually what we called a country club.
So there was, there was no sense to any of it.
It was all senseless, as far as I was concerned.
And they-- killing a, a, a good worker was just the senselessness as, as anything else that happened.
There was no-- I didn't expect any sense or any logic.
And I didn't get any.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Fortunately, the Allies bombed the hell out of Braunschweig.
Were many prisoners killed in the bombing?
We liked it, even, even, even when some of us were killed.
We really enjoyed that.
It was, it was as if somebody was helping us or was trying to help us.
Not, not to my knowledge.
Because they were usually supervised by Germans.
And for the slightest infraction, you could-- you were killed.
It wasn't a matter of a-- you can-- you couldn't appeal to higher authority.
I mean, the, the, the Germans-- I was thinking about it the other day.
The Germans were, were not considered by me to be any higher authority or monsters.
They were more-- they were considered sort of like mean gods, sort of like, like the old Greek writings.
The, the memories-- of course, my father's death-- and there was a, a, there was a little kid.
Sometimes, I blame myself for being guilty possibly of my father's death.
Because there was one kid who became a sort of a homosexual whore around the camp.
And he, he was, he was given food by the kapos particularly.
And it wasn't as if he was compelled to do that by anything except greed.
Sometimes I, sometimes I used to think, if I had done that, maybe my father would have survived.
Well, I didn't do it.
He didn't survive.
And from Braunschweig-- the other great memory that I have of Braunschweig is that little lady dressed in black, throwing me a piece of cake.
I mean, can you imagine what willpower and what indignation it took for her to do anything like that?
She probably would have been punished by the Nazis for doing that.
How did you come to leave Braunschweig?
It's, it's all sort of not quite a blank, but a sort of a, a whirlwind in my mind.
They took us to another camp.
They walked us about 10, 20 miles.
I don't-- it, it was, it was pretty bad.
Because about halfway down there, they made us take our clothes off to have it deloused.
And each one of us had a, a blanket is all.
And then they made us-- we walked to the next camp.
Fortunately at that time, I had acquired a buddy since we knew it would be easier to survive, each one.
I don't remember.
I don't-- and I don't know where he is.
And after the liberation, we completely lost touch of each other.
And I guess he didn't want to keep in touch with me, either.
And on the way down there, we came across a warehouse that had sacks of sugar in it, believe it or not.
Now, sugar was a commodity that was very hard to get in those days.
And we, we tried to load up in-- on sugar, whatever we could eat, of course.
But then we didn't have any clothes where to, to, to take some-- to save some of the sugar.
I went-- I took a, a piece of the blanket and made a sort of a, a pocket in it and filled it up with sugar.
And he and I were eating out of this all the way.
It could-- it might have saved our lives.

And then, and then when we got our clothes back-- by the way, when I say they deloused our clothes, which means they put it in some kind of a, a gas chamber or-- something and you could still see the body lice hanging from the clothes.
We were, we were quite used to it.
I haven't been able to stand bugs ever since.
And we went to several camps from there.
One camp, one camp was Ravensbruck.
That's one of the camps I remember.
But there were a whole bunch of others that I don't even remember the name.
Ravensbruck was a, a huge camp.
It was very disorganized.
And I remember in Ravensbruck, somebody got a hold of a Red Cross package.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And wonder of wonders, we got to look at it.
We couldn't touch it.
Somebody was-- they were supposed to share them among, I think, 20 prisoners.
And I'll never forget that they took out a piece of swan soap, which was beau-- I'd never seen soap beautifully wrapped.
They opened up the, the, the package of soap.
And one guy says, what is it?
And the other guy, I don't know what it is.
But it looks like something good to eat.
And they started chomping down on the, on the swan soap.
And also, that's the first time I ever saw powdered milk.
I didn't get to eat any of it, but I saw it.
Was there-- did you ever notice the presence of the Red Cross or any other relief group?
That's the, that's the only time.

The rumors around camps were when the Germans wanted to show off some of their camps to the Red Cross, they took them to one of their show camps, like Theresienstadt was one of the what we called a country club camp, where the Germans kept up just for the sake of being able to show it.
We, we worked.
Mostly, we were dying.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For instance, the, the most vivid one of these things was the last camp, my last camp.
It sounds ominous-- the last camp-- Ludwigslust.
In northern Germany.
It's spelled L-U-D-W-I-G-L-U-S-T.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was probably the-- my worst camp.
Not that the others were that much better, but my memories of it are worse.
First of all, it was sand.
It was always cold.
And there was a sort of dirty brown sand.
And we were billeted in buildings that had no floors, had no roofs, and had no windows.
And when, when it rained, which was quite often, we were, we were staying in mud most of the time.
In this camp, we were left alone more than in most other camps.
I mean, they didn't supervise us by-- minute by minute or hour by hour.
And when we could, the prisoners would build little fires and roast whatever they could catch.
I think when we came to this camp, there was a large population of rats and cats.
Within several weeks, the rats and cats both were being roasted on those little fires.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in, in, in this camp, my buddy and I organized a jar of oysters that had been thrown into the garbage from the guards' mess, guards' cafeteria.
And we thought we, we'd, we'd really run into some good luck.
Anyhow, we ate it.
And we almost died, because evidently, we didn't know what pickled oysters were supposed to taste like.
But those things were bad.
And after that, it took me 30 years before I touched another oyster.
Anyhow, we ate those oysters with relish.
While one was guarding the other back, the other one ate the oysters, et cetera.
They had a huge pit on one side of the camp.
And every day, they grabbed a bunch of prisoners-- and a lot of people were dying-- to, to take all the corpses and throw them in the pit.
And one day when I was taken to work the corpses and throw them in the pit, I noticed that a lot of the corpses in the pit had pieces of their buttocks missing.
That's when I realized that the roasting that was going on wasn't all rats and cats.
There was some of that going on also.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I tried, I tried to stay away from trading for some of the pieces of roast.
I wasn't into that sort of thing.
Also we got-- we ran into some good luck in that camp, my buddy and I.
About 300 or 400 yards away, there was a train parked.
And the train had food on it.
It had vegetables.
Well, you could see those large cabbages, and kohlrabi that which the Germans absolutely loved, and potatoes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one day, one night, we decided we were going to rob the train.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we sneaked out.
And we didn't see any guards.
They felt so safe.
And we went and tied our pants and our jackets.
And we loaded up with potatoes and all those goodies.
Potatoes were one of the big things that we always craved.
And we start walking down and back to the-- trying to sneak back to the camp from the train.
The train was part of the camp, really.
And there comes a guard with a huge German Shepherd dog.
And I say to myself, Henry, you are absolutely dog meat.
And I wasn't ready to die yet.
And as the, and the guard released the dog when he saw some stuff going on.
And I took a big-- as the dog was jumping at me, I took a huge cabbage with both hands and slammed it at the dog's snout.
And then we made tracks back for camp.
For several days, we ate like kings.
And we were cooking.
We were baking potatoes.
I've never lost my love for baked potatoes.
Well, they're all the same.
It was suffering, and dying, and no food, and occasionally, a little bit of food, and the guards being their nasty selves, even though they knew the war would be-- was going to be over very soon.
Yes, definitely so-- I mean, you look out for my back.
And I'll look out for yours.
